content:
Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 15
Problems of Post-Communism, vol. 59, no. 1, January/February 2012, pp. 15–30.
© 2012 M.E. Sharpe, Inc. All rights reserved. 
ISSN 1075–8216 / 2012 $9.50 + 0.00.
DOI 10.2753/PPC1075-8216590102
An analysis of the coverage of 
post-communist countries by the 
major U.S. television networks 
from 1998 to 2009 reveals that 
U.S. allies receive more positive 
coverage than do non-allies 
or adversaries and that there 
are systematic biases in the 
representation of many post-
Soviet countries. 
The Politics of U.S.  
Television Coverage of  
Post-Communist Countries 
Ivan Katchanovski and Alicen R. Morley
Iv an KatchanovsKI teaches at the school of Political studies, University 
of ottawa. alIcen R. MoRley is a Ph.D. student at the Maxwell school of 
citizenship and Public affairs, syracuse University.
earlier versions of this paper were presented at the Post-communist Politics 
and economics Workshop at harvard University in February 2010, the 2009 
annual Meeting of the american Political science association in toronto, and 
the 2009 annual conference of the canadian Political science association at 
carleton University in ottawa. We are grateful to Babak Bahador, stephen 
Farnsworth, elena Feditchkina, and Roland schatz for their comments and 
suggestions concerning various aspects of this study, and to the Davis center 
for Russian and eurasian studies at harvard University for hosting one of the 
authors to conduct research for this article.
T
h i s study analyzes the coverage of post-communist 
countries, specifically their politics, by the main 
television networks in the United states. It compares the 
extent and the content of the reporting on post-communist 
states such as Russia, Ukraine, Poland, Romania, Bul-
garia, the czech Republic, hungary, Belarus, Kazakhstan, 
Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Georgia, turkmenistan, tajiki-
stan, armenia, azerbaijan, and Moldova. the question is 
whether political factors, such as relations with the United 
states, affect american television coverage of these 
countries. a related research question is whether there 
are significant differences in the extent and the content of 
coverage of former communist states on major television 
news programs in the United states. 
analyzing the coverage of post-communist nations 
by U.s. television networks is important not only from 
a political communications perspective but also from a 
comparative politics and foreign policy perspective. Previ-
ous studies show that media coverage affects the attitudes 
of the american public and the United states’ foreign 
policy agenda toward other countries—in particular, 
Russia (Brewer 2006; norris 2000; semetko et al. 1992; 
Wanta et al. 2004). Western mass-media reporting about 
post-communist countries became an important political 
concern in many of these states. In addition, a growing 
number of politicians and journalists from post-commu-
nist nations, specifically from Russia and Poland, raise 
the question of political biases in the coverage of these 
countries by mass communications media in the United 
states. For example, the inosMI.ru Web site, established 
by the state-owned RIa novosti, specializes in publishing 
online translations of stories from Western newspapers 
and magazines concerning Russia. In 2005, the Russian

16 Problems of Post-Communism     January/February 2012
government launched the Russia today television news 
channel to counter perceived anti-Russian bias in Western 
mass media. 
the first hypothesis tested in this paper is that U.s. 
allies among post-communist countries are likely to re-
ceive much better coverage compared to adversaries and 
non-allies, not only in the areas of relations between these 
countries and the United states but also on other issues, 
including nonpolitical concerns. this relationship is de-
rived from the indexing theory of political communication 
and a theory of american political culture. the indexing 
hypothesis implies that media coverage of U.s. foreign 
policy mostly reflects the framing and political agendas 
of government officials. this mechanism was manifested 
by studying the framing of the Iraq War in U.s. media. 
Prior to and during the Iraq War, the mainstream media 
mostly followed the Bush administration and reported 
their concerns regarding Iraqi weapons of mass destruc-
tion (WMD) and the Iraqi government’s association with 
al Qaeda despite a lack of sufficient evidence (Bennett 
1990; Bennett et al. 2007; hayes and Guardino 2010; 
Mermin 1999).
our study argues that indexing affects the coverage 
of U.s. relations with post-communist states as well as 
of issues such as domestic politics, sports, crime, the 
economy, and immigrants. Because of the strong moral-
istic elements in american political culture, allies of the 
United states are often idealized and represented as the 
embodiment of “good,” while adversaries are presented as 
“evil.” For example, Joseph stalin was turned into “Uncle 
Joe” when the soviet Union and the United states were 
allies during World War II, and his totalitarian rule and 
mass political repression were largely overlooked in the 
United states until the start of the cold war, when stalin 
and the soviet Union were presented in a negative light 
by U.s. officials and the media (levering 1976; lipset 
1996, p. 66). similarly, afghan mujahideen were often 
represented in the U.s. media as “freedom fighters” during 
their war in afghanistan against the soviet Union, despite 
their illiberal Islamist ideology—reflected in their name, 
which is derived from jihad. 
U.s. allies are defined in this study as members of the 
north atlantic treaty organization (nato) or countries 
that pursue pro-Western policies, including the intention 
to join nato. Poland, the czech Republic, and hun-
gary joined nato at the beginning of 1999; Romania, 
Bulgaria, and slovakia were granted membership in this 
military and political alliance in 2004. With the exception 
of the Baltic states, post-soviet countries did not become 
nato members. the governments of Georgia after the 
Rose Revolution at the end of 2003 and Ukraine after the 
orange Revolution in late 2004, however, adopted a pro-
Western orientation in their foreign policy and declared 
nato membership a priority. although nato did not 
offer membership to these states, in april 2008 it did 
recognize Georgia and Ukraine as potential members, 
with U.s. government support. 
In addition, certain non-nato post-communist states 
allied with the United states, to one degree or another, 
during the wars in Kosovo, Iraq, and afghanistan. the 
war in Kosovo in 1999 took place under nato auspices, 
and the U. s. military presence in afghanistan came 
under a formal nato umbrella in 2003. Because these 
tactical alliances were limited to the conflicts in Kosovo, 
Iraq, and afghanistan—and were much shorter in dura-
tion and much less formal, comprehensive, and stable 
than nato—their effect on U. s. television coverage 
of particular post-communist states is likely to be less 
pronounced but still significant. We can expect to see 
evidence of this effect in the news coverage of respec-
tive countries’ roles in these conflicts and changes in the 
coverage of post-communist countries before and after 
these conflicts.
our second hypothesis is that coverage of Russia by 
U.s. television networks is negative. this hypothesis is 
derived from indexing theory, other theories of media 
bias, and previous studies. the political elite in the 
United states has been historically biased against Rus-
sia, in contrast to many other states that were or still are 
communist, such as china (Davis and trani 2009; lieven 
2000a; tsygankov 2009). this bias is likely to manifest 
itself in television coverage of post-communist Russia. 
Most previous studies have found a negative pattern of 
the coverage of this post-communist country. 
our third hypothesis is that coverage of other post-
soviet countries is likely to be affected by the anti-Russian 
bias. the U.s. media often referred to the soviet Union 
as Russia and may still to a significant extent associate 
many post-soviet countries with Russia. 
although bias can be difficult to determine precisely, 
and the concept of bias is often dismissed as too sub-
jective, many studies address various media biases in 
political communications and related fields. Media bias 
is often defined as unbalanced coverage in terms of the 
amount of positive and negative content. D’alessio and 
allen (2000) define the following types of media bias: 
gatekeeping bias, which involves the inclusion or exclu-
sion of certain stories; coverage bias, which relates to the 
relative extent of coverage; and statement bias, which 
deals with favorability of coverage.

Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 17
Media bias is also often defined in terms of the politi-
cal preferences of journalists or their audiences. some 
studies argue that the U.s. media as a whole has a liberal 
bias, because journalists are more politically liberal than 
the general public (Groseclose 2011). other studies 
maintain that significant differences exist in this regard 
among U.s. media. Whereas certain television or cable 
networks—aBc, nBc, cBs, PBs, and cnn—are re-
garded as relatively liberal, others, such as Fox news, are 
relatively conservative. similarly, while public radio sta-
tions like nPR are perceived to be more liberal, talk radio 
is perceived as more conservative. there is an analogous 
dichotomy between more liberal newspapers, such as 
the New York Times and the Washington Post, and more 
conservative counterparts such as the Wall Street Journal 
and the Washington Times (Jamieson and cappella 2008; 
Iyengar and hahn 2009; Morris 2007).
Media bias is also defined as deviation from reality or 
facts. this type of bias is exemplified by the misrepre-
sentation in many U.s. media reports of Iraq as having 
WMD and the Iraq government under saddam hussein 
as having ties to al Qaeda before the onset of the U.s.-led 
war with this Middle eastern country in 2003 (Bennett 
et al. 2007; hayes and Guardino 2010; Kuypers 2006). 
another instance is the portrayal of Russia as an aggressor 
during the Georgian–Russian war by many U.s. media 
outlets, even though Georgia launched the war when it 
attacked its de facto independent breakaway province of 
south ossetia and the Russian peacekeepers stationed 
there (Bahador and Katchanovski 2010; heinrich and 
tanaev 2009). 
Previous Studies
the present article is one of the first academic studies 
to examine the reporting on post-communist countries 
by U.s. television networks. Most previous studies have 
focused on the representation of a single country—Russia, 
Poland, Ukraine, or Romania—in the Western mass media 
(Golan and Wanta 2003; hickman and trapp 1998; Media 
tenor 2004, 2007a, 2007b; norris 1995; Russia 2004; 
semetko et al. 1992). Many studies analyzed coverage 
of these states only in major print media sources in the 
United states and other Western countries (Bohush 2009; 
hickman and trapp 1998). U.s. television networks, how-
ever, reach a much greater audience than newspapers and 
magazines (norris 2000; Pew Research center 2008). 
the absolute majority of previous studies deal with 
various types of media biases concerning domestic issues 
in the United states. For example, previous studies have 
examined various types of U.s. media bias in coverage of 
the Democratic and Republican presidential candidates, 
female presidential contenders, and various racial, ethnic, 
and religious minorities (D’alessio and allen 2000; Falk 
2010). empirical analyses of U.s. television news have 
also reported “home bias” in the amount of coverage 
given to domestic stories compared to stories dealing with 
foreign countries (Pew Research center 1995). studies 
of bias in U.s. coverage of foreign countries since sep-
tember 11 focus on the Iraq War and other conflicts in the 
Middle east or the representation of Muslims and arabs 
by the mass media in the United states and other Western 
countries (Bennett et al. 2007; hayes and Guardino 2010; 
Kuypers 2006; Poole and Richardson 2006). 
content analysis in previous studies has shown that 
Russia receives significantly more coverage in U.s. me-
dia than other post-communist countries do. a sample 
of evening news programs on aBc, cBs, and nBc in 
1988–92 revealed that 17.3 percent of all foreign news 
stories were devoted to the soviet Union or Russia, com-
pared to 2.3 percent to Poland, 2.0 percent to czechoslo-
vakia, and 1.2 percent to yugoslavia (hess 1996, p. 32). 
In January–october 1998, aBc, cBs, nBc, and cnn 
news broadcasts produced 342 stories on Russia, 64 on 
Bosnia, 18 on the Baltic states, and 12 on Poland (Wanta 
et al. 2004, p. 371). 
Both of these studies were conducted in the 1990s. 
they reveal that Russia and the soviet Union before its 
collapse in 1991 received the most foreign coverage from 
the main U.s. television networks. Russia’s prominence in 
the U.s. media declined significantly after september 11 
because of the shifting focus toward Middle eastern coun-
tries, especially Iraq and afghanistan. content analysis of 
evening news programs by aBc, cBs, and nBc, Time, 
and Newsweek between July 2003 and July 2004 showed 
that Russia dropped to the second dozen most covered 
countries in U.s. media. With regard to post-communist 
states, however, Russia remained the leader in terms 
of U. s. news media attention. a much higher number 
(116) of news reports dealt with Russia than with other 
post-communist countries (61). among post-soviet and 
east central european countries, Georgia, Bulgaria, and 
Poland were the subject of the largest number of stories 
(Media tenor 2004).
although Russia receives more attention than other 
post-communist countries, most of the coverage is nega-
tive (Boykewich 2009; lieven 2000a, 2000b). a 1995 
content analysis of television news programs on aBc, 
cnn, csPan, and cBn, two radio talk shows, and the 
New York Times found that coverage of Russia and Bo-

18 Problems of Post-Communism     January/February 2012
ris yeltsin, the Russian president at the time, was very 
negative (Pew Research center 1995). another study 
concluded that much of the coverage of the two wars in 
chechnya in the United states and by the Western media 
in general, “has been relentlessly one-sided and relent-
lessly anti-Russian” ( lieven 2000a). coverage mostly 
focused on the effects of Russian actions on chechen 
civilians, blamed Russia for starting both wars, and largely 
ignored or minimized the role of attacks carried out by 
Islamist terrorists and kidnappers in chechnya at the start 
of the violent conflict in 1994 and its resumption in 1999 
(lieven 2000a, 2000b).
a study of evening news programs on aBc, cBs, and 
nBc from 2002 through the middle of 2004 also reported 
that coverage of Russia was overwhelmingly negative, 
especially on the issues that received the greatest media 
attention—the conflict in chechnya, terrorism, and the 
olympic Games (Russia 2004). content analysis of 
evening news programs on aBc, cBs, and nBc and in 
Time and Newsweek magazines between July 2003 and 
July 2004 showed that the coverage of Russia, Bulgaria, 
and Romania was more negative than the coverage of 
Georgia, the czech Republic, Poland, and Bosnia. With 
the exception of Russia, however, the number of stories 
was too small to make this analysis reliable. For example, 
it included only two stories concerning the czech Re-
public, three regarding Romania, and six about Bulgaria 
(Media tenor 2004). 
a quantitative study, based on computer content 
analysis, shows that post-soviet countries, on average, 
received in 2008, 2009, 2010, and the first quarter of 
2011 more negative coverage in leading global media 
sources than other post-communist states. Russia, which 
had significantly more mentions than all other post-soviet 
states combined, was consistently at the bottom of the an-
nual and quarterly rankings. In 2008–10, Russia ranked 
between 171 and 187 among 200 countries covered by 
the study. In the first quarter of 2011, Russia ranked 185 
on this index. the computer-generated content analysis, 
however, produced a significant variation in the scores 
of many post-communist countries over this short time 
period. It was problematic for this method to distinguish 
the tone of the coverage of Russia and Georgia, which 
waged a war in 2008 over south ossetia. Russia, Georgia, 
and south ossetia all were coded as receiving extremely 
negative coverage in 2008.
1
In contrast, another study found that coverage of both 
Russia and Poland by aBc, cBs, nBc, and cnn in 
1998 was neutral and not much different from the cover-
age of such countries as canada and Italy (Wanta et al. 
2004, p. 373). Most stories about U.s. adversaries (e.g., 
cuba, Iran, and north Korea), however, were also coded 
as being neutral toward these countries. this pattern 
raises questions about the validity of the coding in this 
particular study. 
some academic studies indicate that U.s. news media 
frequently equate Russia with the soviet Union, and this 
misrepresentation extends to post-soviet states. For ex-
ample, american mass media often refer to members of 
organized crime from post-soviet countries as “Russian 
mafia,” even though academic studies reveal that among 
immigrants from the former soviet Union in the United 
states, most criminals are not Russian citizens or ethni-
cally Russian. similarly, organized crime networks in 
Russia include a large proportion of ethnic minorities, pri-
marily chechens, and people from post-soviet countries 
of the caucasus region, especially Georgia (Finckenauer 
and Waring 1998).
commercial firms have conducted a few studies of the 
representation of Ukraine by Western mass media, but 
they have not released all their findings (Bohush 2009; 
Media tenor 2007b). the Western mass media usually 
associate Ukraine with the orange Revolution, political 
instability, the chernobyl nuclear disaster, and conten-
tious relations with Russia (Bohush 2009). U.s. media 
coverage of Ukraine-related companies—as is also true of 
reporting on Poland, croatia, and many other east central 
european and Balkan countries—was generally positive 
in the first half of 2007. this contrasted with mostly nega-
tive american media coverage of Russian and Belarusian 
companies. With the exception of Russia, however, the 
number of reports on Ukraine and other post-communist 
states in that study was extremely small, typically several 
per country (Media tenor 2007b).
the “color revolutions” in Georgia in 2003, Ukraine in 
2004, and Kyrgyzstan in 2005 received significant media 
attention in the West. Previous studies argue that Western 
reporting concerning these political developments has 
been overwhelmingly positive and similar to coverage 
of the velvet Revolution in czechoslovakia in 1989. 
Western media presented mass political demonstrations 
in response to attempts at election fraud as democratic 
pro-Western revolutions (schipani-adúriz 2007). 
Data and Methodology
this study uses quantitative and content analyses of 
news programs about post-communist countries from the 
most-watched U.s. television networks—aBc, cBs, and 
nBc—from 1998 to 2009. We examined the following

Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 19
evening news programs: World News, Nightline, and 20/20 
on aBc; CBS Evening News, CBS Sunday Night News, 
48 Hours, 60 Minutes, and 60 Minutes II on cBs; and 
NBC Nightly News and Dateline on nBc. 
these networks and news programs were selected 
primarily because they have the biggest television audi-
ence in the United states. We excluded other broadcast 
networks such as PBs and cable channels such as Fox 
news, cnn, and MsnBc because their audience is much 
smaller, even though the viewership of Fox news has 
significantly increased since the late 1990s (Pew Research 
center’s Project 2011). For example, the average median 
number of primetime viewers in 2008–10 was 1.9 million 
on Fox news, 0.8 million on cnn, and 0.8 million on 
MsnBc. In comparison, the average median number of 
people watching the evening news during the same time 
period was 7.8 million for ABC World News, 5.9 million 
for CBS Evening News, and 8.6 million for NBC Nightly 
News. the audience for all news magazines included in 
this study also exceeded the total primetime viewership 
for Fox news and other cable news channels. It ranged in 
2008–10 from 3.6 million for Nightline to 11.8 million for 
60 Minutes (Pew Research center’s Project 2011). these 
numbers, derived from data collected by nielsen Media 
Research, are more reliable and precise than self-reported 
survey data. For example, a 2011 Pew Research survey 
produced a different pattern of viewership. thirty and 24 
percent of americans, respectively, said that they watched 
Fox news and cnn regularly, compared to 43 percent 
who viewed the nightly news on the three traditional net-
works combined (Pew Research center 2011, p. 43). the 
exclusion of Fox news may represent a certain limitation 
of this study, since previous research has often reported 
that its coverage differs from the other television channels. 
a previous study, however, found no significant differ -
ences between Fox news and the three leading networks 
in terms of their coverage of the Georgian–Russian war 
(Bahador and Katchanovski 2010). 
this article covers the following post-soviet countries: 
armenia, azerbaijan, Belarus, Georgia, Kazakhstan, 
Kyrgyzstan, Moldova, tajikistan, turkmenistan, Rus-
sia, Ukraine, and Uzbekistan.
2 the Baltic states are not 
included, because they are small and much less likely to 
be associated with Russia than other post-soviet states. 
In contrast to other post-soviet countries, estonia, latvia, 
and lithuania became part of the soviet Union only as 
a result of World War II, and their incorporation into the 
soviet Union was not recognized by the United states. 
Poland, the czech Republic, Romania, Bulgaria, slovakia, 
and hungary are included in this study as post-communist 
countries in east central europe. the ex-yugoslav coun-
tries and albania are excluded from the analysis to keep 
the cold war legacy constant. In contrast to Poland, the 
czech Republic, Romania, Bulgaria, slovakia, and hun-
gary, communist yugoslavia and albania were not soviet 
allies during most or all of the cold war. 
We employed a keyword search of full-text televi-
sion transcripts in the lexis-nexis database to identify 
specific broadcasts and news stories that focused on the 
post-communist countries in the former soviet Union 
and east central europe. For instance, reports found 
with the help of the keywords “Ukraine,” “Ukrainian,” 
and “Ukrainians” are classified as dealing with Ukraine.
3 
only stories that devote fifty or more words to a particular 
post-communist country or people from this country are 
included in the analysis. the reliance on transcripts gives 
an incomplete view of news broadcasts, because informa-
tion presented in visual images is missing. specifically, 
this might affect the coding of the content as positive, 
negative, or neutral. We expect, however, that any poten-
tial biases resulting from reliance on transcripts would be 
uniformly distributed across countries. 
to ensure intercoder reliability, one coauthor coded all 
the data, while the other coauthor coded a large subsec-
tion of data. the data sample, which was coded to check 
reliability, included 618, or 22 percent, of all reports con-
cerning Russia; 247, or 51 percent, on other post-soviet 
countries; and 258, or 52 percent, concerning east central 
european countries. the size of the combined sample is 
large (1,123 stories). It includes either large proportions 
of or all stories dealing with Russia, Ukraine, Poland, 
Romania, Bulgaria, hungary, Georgia, the czech Repub-
lic, armenia, azerbaijan, and Kazakhstan. the overall 
reliability coefficient (alpha) for the entire sample is .75. 
the reliability scores (alpha) are .81 for east central 
european countries, .69 for Russia, and .84 for other 
post-soviet states. each of the double-coded countries 
has similarly high reliability coefficients. 
the study uses comparisons among different countries 
to analyze the quantity and the content of their coverage 
by U. s. television networks. the quantitative analysis 
involves a comparison of the number, the word count, 
and the main subject of news stories dealing with spe-
cific countries. a qualitative analysis undertakes an ex-
amination of the news stories concerning post-communist 
countries in terms of their positive, neutral, or negative 
context. 
stories and major subjects of coverage are coded de-
pending on whether they present a positive or negative 
image of a post-communist country from the perspective

20 Problems of Post-Communism     January/February 2012
of the United states. Reports that present both positive and 
negative sides or are neutral are coded as neutral. 
For example, reports that focus on a post-communist 
country’s relations with the United states and show the 
country to be an american ally, emphasize its pro-U.s. 
foreign policies, or describe visits of foreign officials, 
agreements, cooperation, and so on without significant 
references to tensions, disagreements, or conflicts with 
the United states are coded as positive. conversely, sto-
ries that emphasize adversarial relations with the United 
states, anti-U.s. policies, tensions, disagreements, con-
flicts, and so on are coded as negative. Broadcasts that 
present a post-communist country’s relations with the 
United states in a neutral way or include both positive 
and negative content in similar proportions are treated as 
neutral. (see appendix.) 
similarly, coverage of terrorism is coded as positive 
toward a post-communist country if it focuses on vic-
tims while identifying terrorists as nonrepresentative of 
a country’s population. a focus on government failures 
to prevent terrorist attacks or save hostages, adverse 
consequences of antiterrorist policies, or identification 
of terrorists as representative of a country or immigrants 
from that country is coded as negative. similar coding 
criteria were applied to nonpolitical topics, including 
crime, immigrants, and sport. (see appendix.) 
Main Results
the analysis of television news programs on aBc, cBs, 
and nBc from 1998 to 2009 indicates a bias in terms 
of the amount of coverage of various post-communist 
countries when the size of their population is taken into 
account. It shows that Russia received disproportionate 
coverage compared to other post-soviet countries. the 
number and length of stories dealing with Russia and 
Russians were six and five times higher, respectively, 
than the other eleven post- soviet countries combined, 
even though their population size is similar. this dispar-
ity, however, has declined significantly since 2004. the 
number of Russia-related stories, compared to the other 
post-soviet states, was 3.5 times higher in 2004–9 and 
8.5 times higher in 1998–2003. the difference in the 
length of reports diminished from six to four times. the 
relatively large number of stories dealing with Georgia is 
mostly due to its august 2008 war with Russia. Belarus, 
turkmenistan, and Moldova received very little coverage 
in U.s. television news. (see table 1.)
the post-soviet states, excluding Russia, also received 
less coverage (four-fifths) than their east central euro-
pean counterparts, even though the combined population 
of the post-soviet countries was about 1.5 times greater. 
the disparity in the extent of reporting has not changed 
since 2004, although the disparity in the number of stories 
declined somewhat (t able 1). a similar pattern char -
acterized most individual post-soviet and east central 
european countries with a similar population size. For 
example, Ukraine, Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, and Belarus 
received much less television coverage in 1998–2009 than 
their east central european counterparts with comparable 
populations. Ukraine lagged significantly behind Poland 
on both measures of coverage. the number of television 
reports and the length of the stories concerning Uzbekistan 
and Kazakhstan were considerably smaller than those of 
Romania. similarly, Belarus lagged significantly behind 
hungary, Bulgaria, and the czech Republic. coverage of 
Georgia, armenia, and Kyrgyzstan, however, was more 
extensive than that of slovakia. Many post-communist 
countries, especially Russia, azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, 
Moldova, tajikistan, the czech Republic, hungary, and 
Romania experienced a decline in the number of reports 
and their length from 2004 to 2009 relative to 1998–2003 
(table 1).
In general, american allies among post-communist 
states received relatively more favorable coverage com-
pared to adversaries or non-allies (t ables 2 and 3). on 
average, reporting about countries that had been formal 
U.s. allies since 1999 was more positive (49 percent) than 
reporting about countries that allied with the United states 
in the middle of the last decade (40 percent), and much 
more positive than the coverage of non-allies and adver-
saries (12 percent) (table 2). For instance, when measured 
by the length of broadcasts on various topics from 1998 
to 2009, 66 percent of the coverage on Poland, 48 percent 
on the czech Republic, and 32 percent on hungary was 
positive. In contrast, 0 percent of the reporting on Belarus, 
Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan, and turkmenistan; 
7 percent on Moldova; 11 percent on Russia; 18 percent 
on azerbaijan; 34 percent on armenia; and 48 percent on 
tajikistan was positive. conversely, negativity character-
ized 17 percent of television news reports about Poland, 
12 percent about the czech Republic, and 4 percent 
about hungary—compared to 93 percent on Moldova, 
92 percent on turkmenistan, 86 percent on Belarus, 76 
percent on Kazakhstan, 71 percent on Russia, 66 percent 
on armenia, 65 percent on Uzbekistan, 15 percent on 
Kyrgyzstan, 7 percent on azerbaijan, and 0 percent on 
tajikistan. other coverage was neutral (table 1). 
there is evidence of selectivity bias in the coverage of 
allies versus non-allies and east central european versus

Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 21

post-soviet countries. For example, the main topic of 
television reporting on Poland (58 percent of the whole) 
included overwhelmingly positive reports about Pope 
John Paul II, whose Polish descent and ties to Poland 
were emphasized, and about Polish catholicism. the 
leading czech-related topic in terms of length (31 percent) 
concerned czech immigrants and their descendants. It 
received mostly positive coverage. (see table 4.) Reports 
about the U.s. missile system in Poland and the czech Re-
public focused on the potential benefits of this system to 
U.s. defense capabilities against Iranian or north Korean 
missile strikes. While such political stories as U.s.–czech 
relations, the U.s. missile defense program, the czech 
role in the U.s.-led war in Iraq, and the czech Republic’s 
accession to nato and the eU received mostly favorable 
coverage, they constituted a relatively small fraction of 
reporting on this country from 1998 to 2009. similarly, 
positive reports about hungarian immigrants or people 
identified as having hungarian descent, such as French 
President nicolas sarkozy, constituted the most prominent

22 Problems of Post-Communism     January/February 2012
topic of reporting about hungary since 1998 (21 percent 
of all stories and 14 percent of their overall length). the 
overall coverage of hungary, however, was neutral.
In contrast, reports about some non-allies and the post-
soviet states focused on negative issues. For example, 
discussions about WMD and their potential proliferation 
dominated the negative coverage of Kazakhstan, consti-
tuting 35 percent of the reports. aBc, cBs, and nBc 
news broadcasts concerning armenia and armenians 
also focused on the negative: reporting on armenian 
criminals and terrorists, primarily in the United states. 
Positive portrayals of armenian immigrants and their 
descendants and the issue of the U. s. government’s 
recognition of the genocide of armenians in ottoman 
turkey received less coverage. Reportage on spies, spy-
ing, and the secret services came close to tying first place 
in terms of the length of broadcasts concerning Russia. 
(see tables 5 and 6.)
Romania and azerbaijan, among others, were excep-
tions. Discussions of Romania were dominated by nega-
tive stories dealing with abandoned children and orphans 
(27 percent of the whole) (table 4). In contrast, azerbaijan 
received neutral coverage, dominated by its potential as 
a major oil producer and exporter.
the television coverage of Bulgaria and slovakia be-
came overwhelmingly positive after they joined nato 
in 2004 (table 3). Most reports on Bulgaria were positive 
stories about Bulgarian immigrants in the United states 
and Bulgarian troops and hostages in Iraq. Positive re-
porting about the visit of U.s. President George W. Bush 
characterized broadcasts about slovakia from May 2004 
on. although television reporting about Romania stayed 
mostly neutral after the country joined nato, negative 
coverage declined from 47 percent to 12 percent. cover-
age of “sex slavery” and “sex slaves” (11 percent), Dracula 
(11 percent), and immigrants and their descendants (11 
percent) in Romania was neutral (table 4). For example, 
in contrast to the Borat-related coverage of Kazakhstan, 
news stories about Dracula were neutral in terms of their 
content because, together with the negative image of the 
fictional vampire, they often discussed the invention of 
this stereotype in the West and its positive impact on the 
present-day tourism industry in Romania. 
similarly, the coverage of Georgia and Ukraine be-
came relatively more favorable after the “color revolu-
tions” created pro-U.s. governments in these post-soviet 
states. For instance, positive reporting about Georgia 
from 2004 to 2009 made up 78 percent of broadcasts 
in terms of length, relative to 22 percent in 1998–2003 
(table 3).4 the coverage was dominated by the war 
with Russia in 2008 ( table 5). Georgia was most often 
presented as a small democratic country that suffered 
invasion by Russia. the coverage included several in-
terviews with President Mikheil saakashvili of Georgia, 
who accused Russia of unprovoked aggression against his 
country, and with other Georgians. In contrast, hardly any 
interviews represented the Russian and south ossetian 
sides of the conflict. television news programs on the 
three leading U. s. networks either failed to mention or 
minimized the large-scale Georgian attack that started the 
war on august 7, 2008.5 news reports either ignored the 
Georgian army’s indiscriminate use of force during its 
attack on the south ossetian capital, which resulted in a 
significant number of civilian deaths, or conflated it with 
the Russian counterattack and Georgian civilian casual-
ties. For example, ABC World News Saturday reported

on august 9, 2008, that “hundreds, possibly thousands, 
of civilians [have been] killed, apartment buildings and 
military bases destroyed, as Russian bombers and mis-
siles pound Georgia, a U.s. ally.” 
Positive coverage of Ukraine increased slightly (from 
5 to 12 percent) after the orange Revolution in 2004, 
when this post-soviet state started to pursue a pro-
Western, pro-american foreign policy that included the 
intent to join nato with U.s. backing. negative report-
ing on Ukraine declined more noticeably (from 53 to 40 
percent) (table 3). the leading news topic concerning 
Ukraine focused on the 2004 presidential elections and 
mass demonstrations against election fraud (23 percent of 
stories and 11 percent of total length from 1998 to 2009). 
the coverage included positive representations of viktor 
yushchenko, his pro-Western orientation, and the orange 
forces that supported him. Many reports also discussed 
the government’s falsification of the 2004 presidential 
election results and the potential for conflict to escalate 
in Ukraine because of support of viktor yanukovych 
by pro-Russian forces in Ukraine and by the Russian 
government. the poisoning of viktor yushchenko during 
the 2004 presidential campaign received significant and 
similarly mixed coverage in U.s. television program-
ming (table 5).
american news reports on central asian countries, 
which allowed the United states to use their air bases in 
support of its military operations in afghanistan, were 
relatively more favorable, with the exception of Uzbek-
istan (table 1). For example, tajikistan received some 
of the most positive coverage among the post- soviet 
states. the overall coverage of Kyrgyzstan was mostly 
neutral despite being dominated by terrorism-related stor-
ies. although television reports about Uzbekistan were 
mostly negative and focused on Islamist terrorism, this 
post-soviet country’s supportive role in the U.s.-led war 
in afghanistan received neutral coverage. 
stories about the role of Poland, the czech Republic, 
hungary, and Bulgaria in the Iraq War were positive 
(table 4). these countries joined the U.s.-led coalition 
in Iraq. In contrast, Iraq War–related coverage of Russia, 
which opposed the american invasion, was negative. sim-
ilarly, reports on the Kosovo war involving Russia, which 
opposed the nato military campaign, were negative. 
televised coverage of the role of Russia, which generally 
backed the U.s.-led war in afghanistan, was mixed and 
relatively marginal compared to the other two conflicts 
(table 6). american news programs did not devote much 
attention to the role of other post-communist countries in 
the wars in Kosovo, Iraq, and afghanistan. 
Post-soviet countries generally received more nega-
tive coverage than did post-communist countries in east 
central europe. on average, 16 percent of reporting on 
post-soviet nations was positive and 53 percent nega-
tive, while east central european countries received 46 
percent positive and only 13 percent negative coverage. 
For instance, reporting on Ukraine in 1998–2009 was, 
on the whole, negative. U.s. television programs devoted 
a significant portion of their Ukraine-related coverage 
to criminals, anti-semitism, and negative effects of the 
chernobyl ( chornobyl) nuclear disaster. the second 
leading topic of news reports dealing with Ukraine from

Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 27
1998 to 2009 was crime and criminals. It was the most 
prominent topic from 1998 to 2003, when 27 percent of 
all stories and segments and 21 percent of the total length 
of the coverage dealing with Ukraine was devoted to this 
issue. Positive coverage of Ukraine was mostly confined 
to news reports about sports and athletes (table 5). 
similarly, U.s. television coverage of Kazakhstan was 
largely negative. three leading topics concerning this 
central asian state presented Kazakhstan in negative 
ways. Reporters paid much attention to security issues 
with nuclear and radioactive materials and biological 
WMD left from the soviet period. additionally, one-fifth 
of the coverage was devoted to Borat, a fictional Kazakh 
television journalist played by a British comedian. the 
news programs broadcast stories involving this fictional 
Kazakh reporter and clips from a hollywood movie called 
Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit 
Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan (table 5). 
Belarus, which had adversarial relations with the 
United states, received overwhelmingly negative cover-
age from the major news programs of aBc, cBs, and 
nBc. In addition to reports about criminals and negative 
effects of the chernobyl (chornobyl) accident, aBc’s 
Nightline produced a long broadcast on May 18, 2005, 
that presented as a real possibility a hollywood movie 
plot in which al Qaeda terrorists obtained highly enriched 
uranium from a reactor in Belarus and built a nuclear 
bomb (table 1).
6
Reports on Russia, which was neither an ally nor an 
adversary of the United states, were mostly negative 
from 1998 to 2009. the main topics included relations 
between the United states and Russia (about 15 percent of 
all stories and almost 10 percent of broadcast length), as 
well as spying and the poisoning of alexander litvinenko 
in the United Kingdom (6 percent of the stories and 9 
percent of the word count) (table 6). Most stories about 
U.s.–Russian relations focused on tensions between the 
countries over the U.s. missile system that the Bush ad-
ministration proposed to install in Poland and the czech 
Republic, the Iran nuclear program, and sanctions against 
north Korea. Russian foreign policy was frequently 
presented as aimed at starting a new cold war with the 
United states, allying itself with Iran, invading Georgia, 
and restoring the soviet Union with the use of military 
force. coverage of Russia became mixed, however, after 
relations between the countries improved during the first 
year of Barack obama’s presidency in 2009. 
terrorism in Russia was another leading political news 
topic. the television coverage focused on the takeover 
of a Beslan school by Islamist terrorists in 2004. Many 
terrorist-related reports included largely positive represen-
tations of Russia. For example, they focused on children 
who were taken hostage during the Beslan attack but 
survived. the coverage also featured negative aspects, 
however, such as discussion of Russian government 
failures to prevent terrorist attacks and avoid the deaths 
of several hundred hostages in the Beslan school during 
the rescue operation. 
the third most popular topic in terms of length (9 
percent) dealt with criminals and crime in Russia. news 
reports focused on immigrants from Russia or their 
descendants who were involved or suspected of being 
involved in various crimes. In contrast to the framing of 
hungarian and czech criminals and crime as neutral cases

28 Problems of Post-Communism     January/February 2012
with both negative and positive aspects—for example, 
by focusing on both perpetrators and victims—Russian 
crime was framed negatively. Russian criminals were 
often depicted as members of organized crime groups or 
as threatening to americans, as in the case of computer 
hackers. 
In contrast to the coverage of Georgian actions dur -
ing its attack on south ossetia, news reports about the 
second war in chechnya were overwhelmingly negative 
toward Russia. these broadcasts were mostly framed in 
terms of human rights violations and war crimes by Rus-
sian military forces attacking chechnya and violently 
suppressing the pro-independence movement, destroy-
ing the capital city of chechnya, and killing chechen 
civilians. Reporters minimized or ignored the role of 
radical Islamists in chechnya in attacking the Dagestan 
region and most likely masterminding terrorist apart-
ment bombings in Moscow as reasons for the start of 
the war in 1999. 
the absolute majority of other major political and 
nonpolitical news topics concerning Russia received 
either mostly negative or mostly neutral coverage (table 
6).
7 Reportage emphasized Putin’s reelection as president 
of Russia, the election of Dmitry Medvedev as his hand-
picked successor, the dispersal of demonstrations held 
by the opposition, the detention of opposition leaders, 
government control of the mass media, and killings of 
journalists. Positive stories related to Russia were much 
less prevalent than negative and neutral reports. Positive 
coverage was mostly confined to nonpolitical topics—
immigrants from Russia and their descendants, arts and 
entertainment, and so on (table 6).
More than 5 percent of stories that constituted more 
than 6 percent of the overall length of television reports 
by the major news programs of aBc, cBs, and nBc 
in 1998–2009 explicitly identified the soviet Union as 
Russia or incorrectly associated Russia or Russians with 
the soviet Union. this happened primarily with refer -
ences to the cold war, World War II, the cuban Missile 
crisis, and the soviet war in afghanistan. For example, 
a cBs broadcast incorrectly stated that Russia lost 27 
million people during World War II, although this number 
included casualties in the entire soviet Union. Joseph 
stalin was identified with Russia even though he was 
Georgian. similarly, many reports incorrectly referred 
to the soviet troops in afghanistan as Russian troops. 
In addition, in a number of television broadcasts, other 
post-soviet states, such as Ukraine and Kazakhstan, were 
confused with Russia. For example, Mike Wallace, a co-
host of 60 Minutes, referred to chernobyl (chornobyl) 
as “a Russian town” in the CBS Evening News broadcast 
on March 3, 1999.
the misidentification of Russia with the soviet Union 
is much more extensive than this category suggests. For 
example, many people who were specifically identified 
in television reports as Russian criminals were not Rus-
sian citizens or ethnically Russian. For instance, televised 
reports concerning the figure-skating scandal during the 
2002 Winter olympics named alimzhan tokhtakhunov as 
a “Russian mobster” even though he was born in Uzbeki-
stan. similarly, many television reports referred to ameri-
cans who were recruited by the soviet security services 
but continued to work for Russia after the collapse of the 
soviet Union only as Russian agents. the length of broad-
casts that directly or indirectly misidentified Russia with 
the soviet Union and post-soviet countries from 1998 to 
2009 was comparable to the quantity of reporting about 
Ukraine, Georgia, Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, 
turkmenistan, tajikistan, armenia, azerbaijan, Moldova, 
and Belarus combined. 
Conclusion
our analysis of major aBc, cBs, and nBc news pro-
grams in 1998–2009 shows significant differences in the 
quantity and content of U.s. television coverage of post-
communist countries. Post-soviet states such as Ukraine, 
Kazakhstan, azerbaijan, and Belarus, which became 
independent only in 1991, received disproportionately 
low coverage on U. s. television compared to Russia 
and countries in east central europe relative to their 
population size. this study indicates that one of the main 
factors that accounts for such disparity is the continuing 
association of the former soviet republics with Russia in 
many reports. the length of broadcasts that directly or 
indirectly misidentified Russia with the soviet Union and 
post-soviet countries was comparable to all reportage on 
the other post-soviet states. 
the television coverage of U.s. allies among post-
communist countries—Poland, hungary, and the czech 
Republic—was much more favorable than that on U.s. 
adversaries and non-allies (Belarus, Russia, Uzbekistan, 
and Kazakhstan). american television broadcasts con-
cerning Bulgaria, slovakia, Ukraine, and Georgia became 
significantly more favorable after these countries joined 
nato or adopted pro-U.s. foreign policies. the effect 
of tactical alliances during the wars in Kosovo, Iraq, and 
afghanistan on the coverage of post-communist nations 
was less pronounced but still significant in a number of 
cases.

Katchanovski and Morley  The Politics of U.S. T elevision Coverage 29
the study also indicates a certain selectivity bias in the 
coverage of both allies versus non-allies and east central 
european versus post-soviet countries. With some ex-
ceptions, such as Georgia and tajikistan, the post-soviet 
states received significantly more negative coverage than 
post-communist countries in east central europe. such 
preconceptions against the post-soviet countries can be 
attributed to the effects of the anti-Russian bias. this study 
confirmed findings of several previous studies that reported 
a prevalence of negative coverage of Russia. Broadcasts on 
aBc, cBs, and nBc about Russia from 1998 to 2009 were 
dominated by negative stories, such as tense relations with 
the United states, crime and criminals, spying and secret 
services, and undemocratic developments in Russia. 
this article, in contrast to most previous studies that 
focus on the coverage of a single post-communist country, 
primarily Russia, provides a comparative analysis of eigh-
teen post-communist states. It shows the significance of 
political factors in the representation of post-communist 
countries by american television. U.s. television images 
of post-communist countries are often shaped by such po-
litical factors as these countries’ relations with the United 
states. the study demonstrates biases in the coverage of 
many post-soviet countries by television news programs 
of major american networks. such coverage is likely to 
have real repercussions; for example, in the area of public 
attitudes toward post-communist nations and U.s. foreign 
policy toward post-communist states.

ing to the republic of Georgia. a similar search procedure was used to deal

not related to Poland.

television transcripts.
7. It is noteworthy that traditional stereotypes concerning Russia and 
Russians—vodka and alcoholism, cold climate and siberia, bears, and Russian 
roulette—attracted less television coverage than newly invented stereotypes 
that associate Russia with children adopted in the United states, mail-order 
brides, sex slaves, and oligarchs. With the exception of Russian roulette, a 
fictional game invented by an american writer, the coverage of the traditional 
stereotypes was not negative. For example, vodka, which was presented as a 
Russian invention, received mostly favorable coverage because of its growing 
popularity in the United states (table 6).
